summary: The author reflects on her transition from tech lead to engineering manager, arguing that the skills that make someone a great tech lead don't automatically translate to management success. She describes how her early management effectiveness relied on being hands-on and teaching engineers directly, but as her role grew, she had to develop entirely new skills like running effective 1-1s, identifying systemic issues secondhand, and managing projects she hadn't personally coded. Despite extensive coaching and training, she admits to making many mistakes, and emphasizes that growing a great engineering leadership team requires deliberate structure and support, not just promoting technically skilled individuals.
key_points: Being a great tech lead and being a great manager require fundamentally different skill sets
Most startup managers struggle because they were promoted for technical excellence without learning management fundamentals
Management skills like goal-setting, hiring plans, measuring efficiency, and strategic roadmapping must be explicitly learned
Even experienced tech leads promoted to management need structured support and training to succeed
Companies must create deliberate structures for engineering managers to be successful rather than expecting natural progression
money_quotes: Being a manager is nothing at all like being a tech lead, and hiring managers on the basis of their strength as individual contributors is not the guaranteed way to great technical leadership.
Tech lead stopped being my job long before I learned how to truly manage well.
If your company doesn't provide you with a ton of structure and guidance or experienced managers to learn from, you've got a long road ahead of you if you want to become a great manager.
It is not just about charm and engineering skills, and that is probably how you got here.
You can identify process bottlenecks, but can you identify them when you are not personally impacted by them?
The great tech leads may have an easier time winning over the engineers, but they still need to be taught the basics of structured management, and they won't all be successful or happy in that role.
Engineering management is not just being the tech lead of bigger and bigger teams, and the faster you realize that, the better off your team will be.
key_insight: Engineering management is a distinct discipline from technical leadership, and organizations must deliberately invest in teaching management skills rather than assuming great engineers will naturally become great managers.
